retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue can also be segmented into thirds. The tip constitutes the anterior one-third, while the posterior one-third refers to the tongue base. The middle third lies between the tip and the base. The anterior two-thirds is part of the oral cavity, whereas the posterior one-third, known as the tongue base, belongs to the oropharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral lingual swellings increase in size, eventually merging and overlapping the tuberculum impar. The merger of these two swellings forms the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue originates from the first arch; thus, the sensory innervation to this area is from the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3). Meanwhile, the second, third, and fourth portions of the pharyngeal arch, which make up the copula, develop into the posterior one-third of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue has sensory innervation from the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX), which is a sign that the third arch overlaps that of the second. The third arch derivatives typically are associated with glossopharyngeal sensory innervation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Anterior Triangle
retrieved_docs.content: There are eight branches of the external carotid artery, though only the four most proximal are within the boundaries of the anterior triangle. The superior thyroid artery exits from the anterior aspect of the carotid artery and descends to supply the superior pole of the thyroid gland, the thyrohyoid and cricothyroid muscles, and the internal larynx via the superior laryngeal artery. The ascending pharyngeal artery arises from the posterior aspect of the external carotid, just superior to the superior thyroid artery. This artery dives deep to pierce the pretracheal fascia and supply pharyngeal constrictor muscles. The lingual artery originates from the anterior surface of the external carotid and immediately passes deep to the mylohyoid muscle to supply the muscles of the tongue and the sublingual salivary gland. The facial artery also branches off of the anterior surface of the external carotid just superior to the lingual artery. It then passes deep to the posterior belly of the digastric and stylohyoid muscles and continues its tortuous course superficial to the mylohyoid muscle before hooking over the inferior edge of the mandible just anterior to the masseter muscle. The facial artery supplies the submandibular salivary gland, the soft palate, and many more structures of the face via its numerous branches. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The topography of the tongue encompasses the tip, lateral surfaces, ventral surface, dorsal surface, and base. The tip represents the most anterior part. The lateral edges separate the ventral surface from the dorsal surface. The ventral surface is the underside of the tongue, which opposes the floor of the mouth, while the dorsal surface is the upper surface. The base of the tongue comprises the posterior one-third, including tissue located behind the circumvallate papillae and extending to the vallecula, which is situated between the base of the tongue and the epiglottis. This region is embryologically distinct from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The fibers of the styloglossus muscle can be divided into anterior and posterior bundles. Anteriorly, the bundles run bilaterally and meet at the midline of the floor of the tongue, forming an arch. The posterior bundles of the styloglossus muscle split into approximately 10 smaller bundles of fibers that enter the tongue. These smaller bundles course between the inferior longitudinal intrinsic muscles of the tongue and the genioglossus muscle and ultimately insert into the lingual septum. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal lingual artery arises beneath the hyoglossus muscle and passes to the posterior part of the dorsum of the tongue. It supplies the mucous membrane of this region as well as the glossopalatine arch, lingual tonsils, soft palate, and epiglottis. [9] They anastomose with their corresponding vessels on the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The area of the tongue posterior to the sulcus terminalis is known as the root or base (posterior one-third of the tongue). [5] The body (anterior two-thirds of the tongue) is the largest part of the tongue and extends from the sulcus terminalis to the frenulum. [5] The body is further subdivided into anterior and posterior. The anterior body is the area below the hard palate, and the posterior body is underneath the soft palate. [5] A collection of nodular lymphatic tissue lies towards the posterior one-third of the tongue and is known as the lingual tonsil. The tongue's functions mainly include taste, speech, and food manipulation in the oral cavity. It also plays an essential role in breathing. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Taste sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is supplied via the chorda tympani branch of the facial (VII) nerve and the glossopharyngeal (IX) nerve to the posterior one-third; the glossopharyngeal nerve also supplies somatic sensation to the posterior one-third of the tongue. The lingual nerve, a branch of the mandibular division of the trigeminal (V) nerve, supplies somatic sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Tongue Laceration -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve controls all 8 tongue muscles. Perfusion of the tongue is provided by the paired lingual arteries, which arise from the external carotid system, and venous drainage passes through the lingual veins into the internal jugular veins (see Image . Arteries and Veins of the Tongue). The lingual nerve carries somatic sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is provided by the chorda tympani nerve, whose fibers join the lingual nerve in the infratemporal fossa and continue with them into the tongue itself (see Images . The Mouth Cavity and Chorda Tympani). Taste and somatic sensation from the posterior one-third of the tongue—the base of the tongue—are carried by the glossopharyngeal nerve. The chemoreceptors that provide taste sensation are housed within taste buds, distributed across the tongue's dorsum (see Image . Tongue Papillae and Taste Bud Distribution). For more information on tongue anatomy, please see Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic vessels from the body of the tongue are divided into marginal and central. The marginal vessels drain the tongue's apex, marginal surface, and sublingual region into the submental and submandibular nodes. [10] Less often, they drain into the jugulodigastric or jugulo-omohyoid nodes. The central vessels are located at the tip of the tongue, travel through the mylohyoid muscle, and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid or jugulodigastric nodes. [10] The dorsal vessels collect lymphatic drainage from the area posterior to the sulcus and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid and jugulodigastric nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The muscle has its origin from the inferior surface of the palatine aponeurosis. It descends along with the muscle of the contralateral side in an inferior, forward, and lateral direction anterior to the palatine tonsil and gets inserted into the posterolateral surface of the tongue. Some fibers of the muscle spread over the dorsal surface of the tongue while the other fibers pass deep into the substance of the tongue and intermingle with one of the intrinsic muscles of the tongue (transverses linguae).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is to the submental and submandibular lymph nodes, emptying into the deep cervical chain of lymph nodes. The posterior third of the tongue drains directly into the deep cervical lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is a muscular organ in the oral cavity, which is essential for normal swallowing and speech. [2] Embryologically, the tongue derives from a medial triangular elevation on top of the mandibular arch called the median lingual swelling. [3] It is comprised of skeletal muscle. Motor innervation is via the hypoglossal nerve (except for the palatoglossus muscle, which is supplied by the vagus nerve), while sensory innervation of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue receives innervation from two nerves: the lingual nerve supplying general sensation and the chorda tympani, which provides taste sensation—the posterior one-third of the tongue receives innervation via the accessory nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The suprahyoid artery supplies the omohyoid, sternothyroid, and thyrohyoid muscles. They anastomose with the corresponding vessels from the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The deep lingual artery , which is the termination of the lingual artery, passes between the genioglossus muscle and inferior longitudinal muscle, supplying the apex of the tongue. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Muscles -- The Transverse Muscle
retrieved_docs.content: The transverse muscles extend laterally from the median septum and more anteriorly and posteriorly when compared to the vertical muscles. [5] Contraction of the transverse muscles lengthens and thinners the tongue. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea serves as the anatomical site for tracheostomy procedures, and these procedures may be performed as either temporary or permanent interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: or discectomy. In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.
